name: TRAPPIST-1 b
description: The innermost planet of the remarkable TRAPPIST-1 system — seven Earth-sized worlds orbiting an ultra-cool red dwarf star just 40 light-years away. Discovered using the transit method, this system is one of the best places to search for signs of life beyond Earth.

paragraphs: On May 2, 2016, Michaël Gillon's team at the University of Liège announced that the small 60 cm TRAPPIST telescope in Chile had caught a tiny, repeating dimming in the light of an inconspicuous ultra-cool dwarf star — three Earth-sized planets transiting roughly every 1.5, 2.4, and 4.0 days.

Follow-up with NASA's Spitzer Space Telescope over the next year stitched together what had looked like noise on individual nights into a precise clockwork: not three planets but seven, all rocky, all comparable in size to Earth. Three of them sit in the temperate band where liquid water could exist on the surface.

TRAPPIST-1 b is the innermost — too hot for water, scorched by its red dwarf — but it was the first dip in the light curve that gave the system away. JWST has since begun reading the atmospheres of the outer planets directly, looking for the molecules of habitability.

name: TRAPPIST-1 d
description: A potentially habitable world in the TRAPPIST-1 system with an equilibrium temperature close to Earth's. It's one of the most promising targets for atmospheric characterization with the James Webb Space Telescope.

name: TRAPPIST-1 e
description: Often considered the most Earth-like of the TRAPPIST-1 planets, orbiting squarely in the habitable zone. Its size, mass, and temperature make it a prime candidate for hosting liquid water.

name: 51 Pegasi b
description: The planet that changed everything. In 1995, Michel Mayor and Didier Queloz detected this 'hot Jupiter' orbiting its sun-like star every 4.2 days — the first confirmed exoplanet around a main-sequence star. Their discovery earned the 2019 Nobel Prize in Physics.

paragraphs: Through the autumn of 1994, Michel Mayor and his doctoral student Didier Queloz pointed the ELODIE spectrograph at the Haute-Provence Observatory at a star almost identical to our Sun. They were looking for the tiny, periodic Doppler shifts a planet would impose on its host's light.

What they found, and announced at a Florence conference in October 1995, was impossible by every assumption of the time: a Jupiter-mass planet whipping around its star every 4.23 days, closer than Mercury is to our Sun. No model of planet formation predicted such a thing.

51 Pegasi b was the first exoplanet ever confirmed around a Sun-like star. It rewrote planet formation theory and won Mayor and Queloz the 2019 Nobel Prize in Physics. The radial-velocity method they pioneered has since uncovered more than a thousand worlds.

name: HR 8799 b
description: Part of the iconic HR 8799 system — the first multi-planet system directly photographed. Four massive planets orbit this young star, visible in infrared images as glowing dots. Recent JWST observations revealed CO₂ in their atmospheres.

paragraphs: In November 2008, Christian Marois and a team using adaptive optics on the Keck and Gemini telescopes published infrared images showing not one but three glowing points of light circling the young A-type star HR 8799. A fourth, inner planet was added in 2010.

These weren't transits or wobbles inferred from starlight. These were photons coming from the planets themselves — young, hot worlds still glowing from the heat of their formation, faint enough that a coronagraph and decades of adaptive-optics work were required to separate them from the glare of their star.

HR 8799 became the first multi-planet system ever directly imaged. Recent JWST observations have decomposed the spectra of its four giant planets, finding water, carbon dioxide, and clouds — atmospheric chemistry read across 130 light-years.

name: HR 8799 c
description: The second planet in the HR 8799 system, directly imaged alongside its siblings. At roughly 7 times Jupiter's mass, it's a super-Jupiter still glowing from the heat of its formation.

name: OGLE-2005-BLG-390L b
description: A frozen super-Earth discovered through gravitational microlensing — one of the coldest and most distant exoplanets known. At 50 Kelvin (-223°C), it's a frigid world orbiting a red dwarf 21,000 light-years away, demonstrating that microlensing can find planets conventional methods miss.

paragraphs: In July 2005, the OGLE survey detected a star in the bulge of the Milky Way brightening — a routine microlensing event, where a foreground star's gravity acts as a temporary magnifying glass on a more distant one. Then, riding on top of the smooth magnification curve, came a tiny, brief spike.

That spike was the signature of a planet orbiting the lensing star. Jean-Philippe Beaulieu's PLANET collaboration, alerted by OGLE in time, caught the anomaly across telescopes from Chile to Tasmania. Modelling it gave a planet of about five Earth masses orbiting 2.6 AU from a red dwarf.

OGLE-2005-BLG-390L b sits roughly 21,000 light-years away — one of the most distant exoplanets known, and at 50 Kelvin one of the coldest. Microlensing is the only technique that could have found it; its star is far too dim to study by any other means.

name: HD 176051 b
description: One of only a handful of exoplanets detected via astrometry — the technique of measuring tiny shifts in a star's position caused by an orbiting planet. This Jupiter-mass world was found by tracking its star's wobble with extraordinary precision over many years.

paragraphs: Astrometry — measuring a star's tiny side-to-side wobble as an unseen planet tugs it through space — is the oldest exoplanet detection technique in concept and the hardest in practice. The wobbles are microarcseconds; for decades, every claimed astrometric detection had eventually been retracted.

In 2010, Matthew Muterspaugh's team at the Palomar Testbed Interferometer reported a Jupiter-mass companion to the binary HD 176051, found by tracking the relative motion of its two stars across years of interferometric observations.

HD 176051 b is one of a handful of planets ever confirmed by astrometry. The technique is poised for a renaissance: ESA's Gaia mission, mapping the positions and motions of two billion stars, is expected to unveil thousands of new planets through their gravitational signatures over the next decade.

name: Kepler-22 b
description: The first planet confirmed by Kepler in the habitable zone of a sun-like star. While larger than Earth, its mild temperature could allow liquid water on its surface — making it a milestone in the search for potentially habitable worlds.

name: Kepler-186 f
description: The first truly Earth-sized exoplanet found in the habitable zone of its star. At just 1.17 times Earth's radius, it's the closest we've come to finding an Earth twin, orbiting a cooler, smaller red dwarf star about 580 light-years away.

name: Proxima Centauri b
description: The nearest known exoplanet to our solar system, orbiting the closest star to the Sun. This Earth-mass world sits in the habitable zone of Proxima Centauri, just 4.2 light-years away — making it a prime target for future interstellar exploration.

name: HD 209458 b
description: Nicknamed 'Osiris', this was the first exoplanet observed transiting its star and the first to have its atmosphere detected. Observations revealed sodium, hydrogen, carbon, and oxygen escaping from its atmosphere — a planet literally evaporating under its star's heat.

name: GJ 504 b
description: A directly imaged planet that glows a deep magenta color — one of the lowest-mass planets ever directly photographed around a sun-like star. Its pink hue comes from its extreme youth and heat, making it visible in infrared.

name: OGLE-2016-BLG-1195L b
description: An 'iceball' planet with Earth-like mass discovered via microlensing. At just 37 Kelvin, it's one of the coldest known planets — likely too frigid for life, but it demonstrates that Earth-mass worlds are common throughout the galaxy.

name: Beta Pictoris b
description: A young super-Jupiter embedded in a stunning debris disk around one of the brightest stars in the southern sky. Time-lapse images spanning years have captured this planet actually moving along its orbit — a remarkable feat of direct imaging.

name: 55 Cancri e
description: A 'super-Earth' so close to its star that its year lasts just 18 hours. Surface temperatures exceed 2000°C — hot enough to melt rock. JWST recently confirmed it has an atmosphere, making it the first rocky exoplanet with a confirmed atmosphere.

name: TOI-700 d
description: The first Earth-sized habitable-zone planet discovered by NASA's TESS mission. Orbiting a quiet red dwarf star, it receives about 86% of the energy Earth gets from the Sun — making it one of the most promising candidates for habitability.

name: WASP-121 b
description: An 'ultra-hot Jupiter' so close to its star that it's being stretched into a football shape by tidal forces. Its atmosphere reaches 2500°C — hot enough to vaporize iron and gems. Hubble detected a stratosphere with a temperature inversion, and metals raining from its night side.

name: Kepler-16 b
description: The real-life 'Tatooine' — the first confirmed circumbinary planet, orbiting two stars. From its surface, you'd see a double sunset. This Saturn-mass world proved that planets can form and survive in the complex gravitational environment of a binary star system.

name: Gliese 667 C c
description: A super-Earth in the habitable zone of a triple star system. From this world, you'd see three suns in the sky. Its equilibrium temperature of ~4°C is remarkably Earth-like, making it one of the most intriguing nearby habitable-zone planets.
